behaviors.description: Files in subdirectories of the watched root are processed, not only files in the root directory
behaviors.justification: Real filesystem watching requires testing against actual directory structures. A Unit mock of fs.watch could pass even if the code fails to enable recursive watching or properly traverse subdirectories. Only Integration testing with real nested directories and actual file placement can verify the watcher correctly monitors nested paths.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a temporary directory tree with nested subdirectories, start the file watcher, create a file inside a subdirectory (e.g., `watched_root/sub/file.txt`), and assert that the file is processed and output appears in the output directory.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test creates real directories and files in subdirectories, requiring Integration infrastructure to verify actual recursive watching behavior works.

behaviors.description: Deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window
behaviors.justification: The deduplication logic is independent of actual filesystem watching. By mocking duplicate events with controlled timing, a Unit test can verify the filter logic correctly suppresses duplicates within the time window. The integration with the actual watcher (behavior #3) is tested separately.
behaviors.test_description: Emit two identical filesystem events for the same file path within the configured time window (e.g., 100ms) via a mock event source, and assert that the file is queued for processing exactly once, proving the deduplication filter suppresses the second event.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses mocked events only, which is appropriate for Unit-level testing of the deduplication filter logic without filesystem I/O.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once
behaviors.justification: While dedup logic alone is Unit testable, verifying it handles real OS-level duplicate events requires Integration testing. OS behavior (emitting multiple events for a single write) cannot be reliably simulated at Unit level—only actual filesystem operations or realistic OS event simulation can trigger the exact conditions this behavior targets.
behaviors.test_description: Write a file through real filesystem I/O in a way that triggers or simulates multiple write events from the OS (e.g., writing in chunks or monitoring raw OS events), observe the daemon's processing queue, and assert the file appears for processing exactly once despite multiple underlying OS events.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses real filesystem I/O to generate duplicate OS events, requiring Integration infrastructure to simulate the actual OS event behavior that deduplication must handle.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart
behaviors.justification: Error recovery when the watched directory is deleted requires real filesystem state changes. Unit testing cannot simulate the OS error that fs.watch throws when the watched directory disappears. Integration testing with actual directory deletion/recreation is necessary to verify the daemon gracefully handles the error and resumes watching.
behaviors.test_description: Start watching a directory, delete the watched directory on the filesystem (simulating backup rotation or cleanup), verify the daemon handles any errors without crashing, recreate the directory, place a new file in it, and assert the daemon processes the file, confirming the watch resumed without manual restart.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires actual filesystem operations (directory deletion/recreation) to trigger real OS errors and recovery behavior, requiring Integration infrastructure.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps
behaviors.justification: The ordering logic is independent of filesystem operations. By providing mocked file metadata with various creation timestamps (including out-of-order arrivals), a Unit test can verify the ordering algorithm correctly sorts by timestamp. Actual filesystem access is unnecessary to test this sorting behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Queue multiple files for processing via mocked events with distinct creation timestamps provided out of order, and assert the processing pipeline orders them by creation timestamp (not arrival order) before executing the transformation pipeline.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses mocked file metadata only, which is appropriate for Unit-level testing of the ordering algorithm without requiring filesystem I/O.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete
behaviors.justification: File write completion detection depends on filesystem state (file size, modification time, file handle status). Real file I/O is necessary to test this. Unit testing cannot simulate the actual in-progress write state and completion conditions that the code must detect. Integration testing with gradual file writes is required.
behaviors.test_description: Open a file handle and write partial data to a file, verify the daemon does not process the incomplete file, then write remaining data and close the handle, and assert the daemon processes the file after completion is detected.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real file I/O and gradual writes to verify write completion detection, requiring Integration infrastructure.
